alternative_names: Shri Digamber Jain Siddha Kshetra Kundalpur
canonical_or_religious_significance: In Jain tradition, Moksha Sthana - place of salvation for Shridhar Kevali
historical_significance: Important pilgrimage site for Jains in Damoh district of Madhya Pradesh. Lies 37 kilometers northeast of Damoh city. Home to numerous Jain temples including renowned Bade Baba Temple dedicated to Rishabhanatha. Bade Baba Statue most prominent feature standing atop hill in striking 15-foot form of Rishabhanatha seated in padmasana on 3-foot pedestal. Central figure flanked by two statues of Parshvanatha of equal height. Bade Baba Temple most renowned of Kundalpur's 64 temples. Originally established during 8th century CE houses ancient idol dating back to post-Gupta period. Temple complex rediscovered by Bhattaraka Surendrakirti of Mulasangha-Balatkaragana-Sarasvati Gachchha lineage who with support from Bundela ruler Chhatrasal led its reconstruction. Starting in 1997 construction began on new larger structure incorporating elements of Nagara and modern Rajasthani architectural styles. Total of 2633 images consecrated. Spread across 1000 acres.
approx_established_period: 8th century CE, renovated 1700 CE, new temple 1997

canonical_or_religious_significance: Ancient Siddha and Atishaya Kshetra; in Jain tradition, believed about 3.5 crore munis attained salvation from this place
historical_significance: Ancient Siddha and Atishaya Kshetra located on border of Madhya Pradesh and Maharashtra. Comes under Bhainsdehi tehsil of Betul district. 52 temples on mountain surrounding beautiful waterfall. First temple of lord Parshwanathji model of architecture. Idol ancient and glorified with seven snake hoods. In devotional tradition, believed sandal-saffron shower obtained on every Ashtami Chaudas and poornima. Most temples built in 16th century and later on. Temple no. 10 famous as Mendhagiri which carved in mountain itself very ancient one built in 10th century. Design and structure of pillars roof worth to watch. Main temples are 1st Temple, 10th Temple, 26th Temple and 40th Temple. Main temple Temple no. 26 dedicated to 23rd Tirthankar Bhagwan Parshwanath. In tradition, Murti believed to be 3000 years old.

Located in eastern cluster of world-renowned Khajuraho Group of Monuments in Madhya Pradesh. Built between 9th and 12th centuries CE under illustrious Chandela dynasty. Temples dedicated to revered Tirthankaras Parshvanatha, Adinatha and Shantinatha. Represent spiritual devotion and mark zenith of Nagara style of temple architecture. Form vital part of UNESCO World Heritage Site of Khajuraho. Epigraphic evidence and historical accounts suggest at cultural peak Jain enclosure at Khajuraho housed over 30 temples of which about 22 structures still survive. Parshvanatha Temple largest and most elaborate Jain shrine at Khajuraho built around 950-970 CE. Though originally dedicated to Adinatha later rededicated to Parshvanatha. Shantinatha Temple most active and significant Jain shrine in Khajuraho today built between 950-1050 CE extensively reconstructed in 19th century. Sanctum enshrines 13-foot-high seated image of Shantinatha carved from sandstone.

alternative_names: Shri Digambar Jain Siddha Kshetra Aaharji
historical_significance: Located at Village Baldevgarh in Tikamgarh district of Madhya Pradesh. Very ancient holy place important from historical and archaeological aspects. Built during Chandella dynasty. Jain idols recovered from land and ancient remains of Jain temples present in huge quantity. Excavations found large number of Jain images installed here during 954 to 1275 AD spanning reigns of six Chandella rulers. Major Jain center during Chandella period. Inscriptions from 1180 AD mention 32 different Jain communities that funded construction. Inscriptions on rocks mention time of Vikram Samvat 1100-1500. Main temple of Shri 1008 Shantinath Bhagwan built in 12th century and idol of Mulnayak Bhagwan Shantinath installed in Vikram Samvat 1237. Bhagwan Shantinathji main murti approximately 18 feet high carved in standing position. Along wall Trikal Chaubisi and 20 Tirthankaras of Videha Kshetra images installed. Murtis from 11th and 12th century. At site Siddho ki Toriyan, Siddho ki Gufa, Siddho ki Maniyan, Jhalar ki Toriya important places proving ancient sacred place. Total 12 temples including Panch Pahari.

canonical_or_religious_significance: In Jain tradition, place where Samavsharan of Bhagwan Chandraprabhu came 17 times; place where many saints including Nang, Anang, Chintagati achieved salvation
historical_significance: Located about 65 km from Gwalior in Datia district of Madhya Pradesh. Has scores of Digambar Jain temples dating from 9th century onwards. Popular among devotees and ascetic saints to practice self-discipline austerity and attain Moksha. Known as Laghu Sammed Shikhar covering area of 132 acres of two hills. 77 beautiful Jain temples on hills and 26-31 temples in village. Temple no. 57 on hill main temple with attractive artistic spire. In this temple principal deity Lord Chandraprabhu eleven feet in height. In tradition, main Pratima ji 2000 years old. Two other beautiful idols of Lord Sheetalnath and Parshvanath installed. Has Manastambh 43 feet in height and attractive model of Samavsharan. In Jain belief, Samavsharan of Bhagwan Chandraprabhu came here for 17 times. In tradition, many saints achieved salvation from here including Nang, Anang, Chintagati, Poornachand, Ashoksen, Shridatta, Swarnbhadra.

Beautiful Digambar Jain temples situated at Gwalior. Famous for golden work done on roof and walls of temples. Temples amazingly constructed and very beautifully designed. Construction of main temple completed in 1704. Till present day Swarn Jain Mandir glorifies its presence being one of oldest Jain temples in Gwalior. Important Jain pilgrimage center in Madhya Pradesh.

Historical site located at Nidanpur near Chanderi 19 km from Chanderi in Madhya Pradesh. Site consists of ruins of around 55 Jain temples most of which in ruins. Temples believed to have been built between 9th-11th centuries by Pratihara kings. Architecture similar to that of Badoh Pathari Jain temple. Some structures possibly saw restoration during Bundela period as some superstructures have features of Bundela architectural style. Important heritage site in Madhya Pradesh.

One of most significant Jain temples in India located in historic town of Chanderi Madhya Pradesh. Temple dedicated to first Tirthankara of Jainism Lord Adinath. Built in time when Jainism flourishing in region evident from numerous Jain temples that dot landscape of Chanderi. Temple's history as ancient as town itself which dates back to 11th century. Temple dateable to 12th century CE. Many temples constructed during period majority belonging to 950-1150 CE and few to 1150-1300 CE. Village became important pilgrimage center with establishment of monasteries both Shaiva and Vaishnava. Thobon was great Jain pilgrimage center. Village retains this character at present and as Thubonji considered Jain Atishaya Kshetra with Jain trust managing temples and activities.

Historic Jain temple located near Jabalpur Madhya Pradesh. Approximately 650-660 years old. In tradition, temple built by Pisanhari Dai (old woman who ground grain) who chose this mountain for temple. Two main pratimas said to have been installed by Pisanhari Dai with inscription below indicating Samvat year when idols placed in temple. After temple built region started developing. Temple managed by trust committee. Development works continue since Pisanhari Dai started construction 650 years ago till 2024. Important Jain pilgrimage site in Madhya Pradesh.

Group of 31 Jain temples located at Deogarh in Lalitpur district Uttar Pradesh built around 8th to 17th century CE. Protected by Archaeological Survey of India. ASI maintains archaeological museum at Deogarh site noted for treasured archaeological sculptures. Fort temples dominated by Jain temples in eastern part of hill fort. Complex consists of 31 Jain temples housing around 2000 sculptures which are largest such collection in world. Jain temples have large number of panels depicting scenes from Jain mythology tirthankara images and votive tablets. Pillars carved with thousand Jain figures. Idol of Bhagawan Shantinatha nearly 12 feet 6 inches tall and light red in color seated in Padmasana posture. Site notable for many stone inscriptions from 8th to 15th century including unique inscription in eight languages.

Ancient temple located in Gyaraspur village in Vidisha district of Madhya Pradesh. Important archaeological site with Jain heritage. Part of historic village with medieval monuments. Site has ancient Jain temple ruins showing rich Jain culture in region.

Ancient Jain caves located in Pachmarhi hill station in Hoshangabad district of Madhya Pradesh. Caves used by Jain monks for meditation and religious practices. Part of rich heritage of Pachmarhi region. Important archaeological site showing ancient Jain presence in Central India.
